Made significant progress on MIRA hosted version during two-month development cycle with extensive changes to both frontend and backend
Building MIRA, a long-term application project. Currently in constant cycle of build-cleanup-build-cleanup involving architectural fixes, bug fixes, and redesigning deprecated components.
On two-day timeline to complete documentation and tests for MIRA open source release, targeting 0.9 quality (ship it when it works, not pursuit of perfection)

Chose not to open source MIRA prematurely because early release would allow venture-backed companies to exploit the codebase and add engagement-maximizing features that contradict the core mission, plans to establish significant traction first
MIRA's interface is now working well and 'really clicking' after iterative development and troubleshooting
Discovered significant bugs in MIRA through community usage on codepaths not explored during solo development, resulting in much more robust software
Planning OSS release of MIRA on the 31st
Currently working toward OSS release for MIRA, planning to use release as line in sand for architecture stability rather than continued rearchitecting.

samples.memory_text: Regularly rides Tail of the Dragon in North Carolina - appreciates the 318 curves in 11 miles and technical nature of the road, but also values the contrast with rural Fontana Lake area and mountain roads past Fontana Dam for decompression
samples.implication_text: choosing trail routes that balance technical sections with scenic decompression zones; adjusting ride plans to include multiple stops for mental rest after technical sections; prioritizing routes that offer both high-intensity riding and natural tranquility; scheduling rides to avoid peak traffic hours near Fontana Lake for smoother transitions between sections

samples.memory_text: Solo developer on MIRA project - carries all responsibility for architectural decisions, debugging, and maintenance. Experiences fatigue from constant rearchitecting cycle without shared load.
samples.implication_text: choosing a modular architecture to reduce rearchitecting cycles; delegating code reviews to a peer; implementing automated testing to catch regressions early; scheduling regular breaks to prevent burnout; documenting architectural decisions to share knowledge; using a CI/CD pipeline to automate deployments; setting clear deadlines for each architectural phase; pairing with another developer for complex problem solving; creating a feedback loop with stakeholders to align on priorities; limiting scope of each architectural iteration to avoid over-engineering

implication_candidates.text: After architecture and mental model design phase is complete, remaining implementation work is deterministic engineering to bridge gap between architectural intent and code reality, not intellectual work

Architecture and mental model design is the hard cognitive work; implementation details like fixing SQL commands and federation adapters are deterministic engineering to bridge gap between architectural intent and code reality
Works hard in own business while developing programming projects, balancing commercial work with creative technical development
